beats.activity: Wake up, shower, and morning coffee at home.
beats.reasoning: Amara starts every day with a swim, but this beat is the immediate wake-up routine; the swim happens next. Full energy on day one.

beats.activity: Early morning swim at the pool.
beats.reasoning: Amara can't start the day without a swim; Ballard Pool is a reliable early-morning option and sets her up energized for work.
From Ballard to First Hill is a 25–30 min drive; Light Rail closure (9am–3pm) doesn't affect her early arrival.

beats.activity: Morning casework and client meetings at the nonprofit.
beats.reasoning: Standard workday start; Amara focuses on casework before the 11am FIFA match, which she may or may not attend depending on workplace mood.

beats.activity: Lunch and USA vs Wales FIFA match at a downtown bar.
beats.reasoning: Amara is blunt and direct; she'd catch the 11am match at the exact start time if interested. Downtown bars are open early for the World Cup. She's adventurous about food and social, so a market lunch with the game fits her personality.
Short commute back from Downtown; allows time to recharge before afternoon casework.

beats.activity: Afternoon casework, client check-ins, and administrative tasks.
beats.reasoning: Full afternoon of social work; typical workday rhythm for a nonprofit social worker focused on youth services.
Drive from First Hill to West Seattle; ~30 min in early evening traffic. Light Rail closure doesn't affect her.

beats.activity: Quick errand: pick up groceries for the week at a local market.
beats.reasoning: Amara values adventurous eating and likely meal-plans; a quick Monday errand before dinner fits her organized, direct personality.

beats.activity: Prepare and eat dinner at home; relax with a book or news.
beats.reasoning: Wind-down at home on a sunny Monday; Amara is already at home for the sleep beat. Values close friendships over big crowds, so an evening at home suits her energy after a full workday.
9.5-hour overnight sleep; ends at 06:00 the next day, visible as a span across midnight on the map replay.

diary: Started the day right with my swim at Ballard Pool—the water was perfect, cool and quiet at that hour. Got to the office and worked through a stack of intake forms and a couple of difficult check-ins with kids who are struggling. Around 10:30, I caught wind that the USA was playing Wales in the World Cup this morning, and honestly? I needed the break. Ducked down to Pike Place and grabbed a seat at one of the bars with a sandwich. The energy was electric—everyone crowded around the screens, and for an hour I was just another person in the crowd, not thinking about case files or budget cuts. Got back to the office a bit revived, powered through the afternoon appointments. By the time I hit Safeway on the way home, my feet were sore but my head felt clearer. Made a simple dinner, cracked open my book. There's something about a Monday that starts with water and ends with quiet.
thought_process: I needed that swim this morning—it centers me before the emotional labor of the day. The Light Rail closure didn't affect my route, which was lucky. Around mid-morning, I felt the weight of the casework settling in, and when I heard about the match, something in me just said go. I'm not usually one to duck out for sports, but I think I needed permission to be just a person for a bit, not the social worker. The bar scene recharged me enough to handle the afternoon without burning out. The weather helped too—sunny, warm, made everything feel a little less heavy. By evening, I was content to be home and still, which tells me I made the right calls today.
world_event_prompt: Sunny Monday in early June, high of 74F. FIFA World Cup group stage match (USA vs Wales) airs at 11am PDT — bars downtown open early. Light Rail closed between Westlake and University District 9am-3pm for maintenance.
